meta.title: Scala
meta.description: Scala applications with functional patterns, Akka, and big data (Spark)

items.text: Are vals preferred over vars, and immutable collections used by default?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use val instead of var. Use immutable collections (List, Map, Set) from scala.collection.immutable by default.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mutable state causes concurrency bugs and makes code harder to reason about. Immutability prevents entire classes of bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for var declarations. Check imports for scala.collection.mutable. Verify mutability is justified (performance-critical code).
baseContent.exampleComment: This var is only assigned once after declaration. Use val with a single expression instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to val and immutable collections. Use var only when mutability provides a clear benefit.

items.text: Is Option used correctly instead of null, with map/flatMap/getOrElse?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Option[T] instead of nullable T. Process Options with map, flatMap, fold, or getOrElse — not .get or pattern matching on every access.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using .get on None throws NoSuchElementException. Using null defeats Scala's type safety. Excessive pattern matching is verbose.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for .get on Option values. Check for null usage. Look for nested pattern matching that could be flatMap chains.
baseContent.exampleComment: user.name.get will throw if name is None. Use user.name.getOrElse("Unknown") or map over it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never call .get on Option. Use map/flatMap/getOrElse for safe access.

items.text: Are Either and Try used for error handling instead of exceptions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Either[Error, Result] or Try[Result] for operations that can fail. Reserve exceptions for truly exceptional conditions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Exceptions bypass the type system and are invisible in function signatures. Either/Try make failure cases explicit and composable.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for try/catch blocks that handle expected failures. These should use Either or Try instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: This catches NumberFormatException to handle invalid input. Use Try(value.toInt) or value.toIntOption instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Either/Try for expected failures. Reserve exceptions for programmer errors and unrecoverable failures.

items.text: Are for-comprehensions used appropriately for monadic composition?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use for-comprehensions to compose Option, Either, Future, or other monadic types. Don't use them as fancy loops.
baseContent.whyItMatters: For-comprehensions make monadic chains readable. But mixing different monad types or using them for simple maps adds confusion.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that for-comprehensions combine the same monad type. Verify they aren't overused for simple map/filter operations.
baseContent.exampleComment: This for-comprehension mixes Option and Try types. Use flatMap to explicitly convert between them or normalize to one type.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use for-comprehensions for chaining operations on the same monad type.

items.text: Are sealed traits/enums used for algebraic data types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use sealed traits (Scala 2) or enums (Scala 3) for types with a fixed set of variants. The compiler checks exhaustiveness.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unsealed traits allow anyone to add variants, breaking pattern matching. Sealed traits enable exhaustive compiler checks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check pattern matches on trait hierarchies. If all variants are in one file, the trait should be sealed.
baseContent.exampleComment: This trait has only 3 implementations, all in this file. Seal it so the compiler warns about non-exhaustive matches.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Seal traits when all variants are known. Let the compiler catch missing cases in pattern matches.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Implicits should be defined in companion objects, package objects, or clearly named implicit objects. Avoid scattered implicits.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hard-to-find implicits make code impossible to understand. Developers can't tell what conversions or instances are in scope.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check where implicit values/conversions are defined. Verify they're in standard locations (companion objects, dedicated implicits trait).
baseContent.exampleComment: This implicit conversion is defined in a random utility object. Move it to the companion object of the target type.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Put implicits in predictable locations. Companion objects and dedicated implicit objects are standard.

items.text: Are implicit conversions avoided (use extension methods instead)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Implicit conversions are dangerous and deprecated in Scala 3. Use extension methods or explicit conversions instead.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Implicit conversions hide type changes, cause surprising behavior, and make debugging extremely difficult.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for implicit def that takes one parameter and returns a different type. These are implicit conversions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This implicit conversion from String to UserId is dangerous. Use an explicit UserId.fromString() method instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid implicit conversions. Use extension methods (Scala 3) or enrichment pattern (Scala 2) instead.

items.text: Is the Scala 3 given/using syntax used instead of implicit (if on Scala 3)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Scala 3 replaces implicit with given/using for clearer intent. given defines instances, using requests them.
baseContent.whyItMatters: given/using separates implicit definitions from implicit parameters, making code clearer and easier to understand.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for Scala 2-style implicit keyword in Scala 3 code. Verify new code uses given/using.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses 'implicit val' in a Scala 3 project. Use 'given' instead for the idiomatic Scala 3 syntax.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use given/using in Scala 3. Migrate from implicit during refactoring.

baseContent.whatItMeans: CPU-bound work should use a sized thread pool. Blocking I/O should use a separate blocking execution context, not the global one.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Running blocking I/O on the global ExecutionContext starves CPU-bound futures. All async operations slow down or deadlock.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for Future { blocking { ... } } without a dedicated blocking EC. Verify ExecutionContext.global isn't used for I/O.
baseContent.exampleComment: This blocking database call runs on ExecutionContext.global. Use a dedicated blocking EC to avoid starving the thread pool.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use separate ExecutionContexts for CPU-bound and I/O-bound work. Never block on the global EC.

items.text: Are Akka actor messages immutable case classes/objects?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Actor messages must be immutable. Use case classes and case objects. Never send mutable objects between actors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mutable messages shared between actors cause race conditions. The actor model's safety depends on message immutability.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check message types for var fields or mutable collections. Verify messages are case classes/objects.
baseContent.exampleComment: This message contains a mutable ArrayBuffer. Use an immutable List instead to prevent concurrent modification.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Actor messages must be immutable case classes. Never share mutable state between actors.

items.text: Are Akka actor supervision strategies configured?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Actors should define supervision strategies (Resume, Restart, Stop, Escalate) based on child failure types.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The default strategy may not be appropriate. Without proper supervision, failures either crash the system or are silently ignored.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that parent actors define supervisorStrategy. Verify the strategy matches the failure semantics of child actors.
baseContent.exampleComment: This parent actor has no supervisorStrategy. Child failures will use the default (restart). Define an explicit strategy.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define explicit supervision strategies. Match the strategy to the failure characteristics of child actors.

items.text: Is the ask pattern used with appropriate timeouts?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The ask pattern (?) creates a temporary actor for responses. Always set a timeout. Prefer tell (!) when a response isn't needed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Ask without timeout leaks actors if the target never responds. Overuse of ask defeats the async benefits of actors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for ask (?) patterns. Verify timeouts are set. Consider if tell (!) with a callback is more appropriate.
baseContent.exampleComment: This ask has no implicit timeout. Add implicit val timeout: Timeout = Timeout(5.seconds) or use tell with a response actor.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always set timeouts on ask. Prefer tell when you don't need a response.

items.text: Are closures and objects sent to executors serializable?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Spark ships closures to executor nodes. Any object referenced inside a closure must be serializable.
baseContent.whyItMatters: NotSerializableException is the most common Spark error. It crashes jobs at runtime, often after expensive computation.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check closures in map/filter/flatMap for references to non-serializable objects (connections, SparkContext, unserializable classes).
baseContent.exampleComment: This map closure references a database connection. Connections aren't serializable. Use forEachPartition and create connections inside.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Only reference serializable objects in Spark closures. Create connections inside partition-level operations.

items.text: Is data partitioning appropriate for the workload?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Data should be partitioned by key for joins and aggregations. Too few partitions waste parallelism, too many create overhead.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong partitioning causes data skew (one partition with 90% of data) or excessive shuffle (repartitioning on every operation).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check repartition() and coalesce() calls. Verify partition count matches cluster resources. Watch for skewed keys.
baseContent.exampleComment: This join repartitions both DataFrames. Pre-partition by the join key to avoid shuffles: df.repartition(col("user_id")).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Partition by join/aggregation keys. Size partitions to match cluster resources (2-4x cores).

items.text: Is collect() avoided on large datasets?

baseContent.whatItMeans: collect() brings all data to the driver. On large datasets, this causes OutOfMemoryError on the driver node.
baseContent.whyItMatters: collect() on a billion-row DataFrame tries to load it all into driver memory, crashing the application.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for .collect(). Verify the data is guaranteed to be small. Use .take(n) or .show() for debugging.
baseContent.exampleComment: This collects the full result DataFrame. With 10M rows, this will OOM. Use .write to save results or .take(100) to sample.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never collect() large datasets. Use write operations or take() for sampling.

items.text: Are RDDs/DataFrames cached only when reused?

baseContent.whatItMeans: cache()/persist() keeps data in memory across operations. Only cache data that's accessed multiple times.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unnecessary caching wastes executor memory, potentially causing spills to disk or OOM errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for .cache() or .persist() calls. Verify the cached data is actually used more than once. Call .unpersist() when done.
baseContent.exampleComment: This DataFrame is cached but only used once. Remove .cache() to free executor memory for other operations.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Only cache DataFrames used multiple times. Unpersist when done.

items.text: Are tests using a consistent ScalaTest style?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Pick one ScalaTest style (FlatSpec, FunSuite, WordSpec, etc.) and use it consistently. Mix in appropriate matchers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mixing test styles makes the test suite inconsistent and harder to read. Different styles have different conventions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that new tests use the same ScalaTest style as existing tests in the project.
baseContent.exampleComment: Existing tests use FlatSpec but this new test uses FunSuite. Use FlatSpec for consistency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use one consistent ScalaTest style throughout the project.

items.text: Are property-based tests used for pure functions with complex inputs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use ScalaCheck for property-based testing of pure functions. Define properties that hold for all valid inputs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Example-based tests only cover cases you think of. Property-based tests find edge cases automatically.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for pure functions with complex input domains. These are good candidates for property-based testing.
baseContent.exampleComment: This serialization/deserialization pair is a good candidate for property testing: forAll { (x: T) => deserialize(serialize(x)) == x }.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use property-based tests for serialization, parsing, and mathematical functions.

items.text: Are SBT dependencies correctly scoped and versioned?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dependencies should use the correct scope (% vs %%, Test scope). Cross-version suffixes (%%) must match the Scala major version.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong dependency scope includes test libraries in production. Wrong cross-version suffix causes NoClassDefFoundError at runtime.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for test libraries without % Test scope. Verify %% is used for Scala libraries and % for Java libraries.
baseContent.exampleComment: scalatest is not scoped to Test. Add % Test to avoid shipping test code to production.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Scope test dependencies with % Test. Use %% for Scala libraries, % for Java libraries.
